retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Technical Elements of Images Acquisition - Technical acquisition of radiographs depends on the anatomic body part being imaged. Radiology studies are generally comprised of 3 or more views; however, a detailed description of patient positioning and required views for specific radiographic studies is beyond the scope of this topic. In general, musculoskeletal examinations comprise anteroposterior, lateral, and oblique views with additional unique views for various body parts such as the Grashey and axillary views of the shoulder or the Judet view of the pelvis. The following represent basic instructions for classic anteroposterior (AP) views of common anatomic studies obtained in radiology with collimation values adapted from the 14th edition of Merril’s atlas of radiographic positions and radiologic procedures:

retrieved_docs.title: Dual-Energy X-Ray Absorptiometry -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no absolute contraindications for performing DEXA. The possibility of inadequate studies or the need for modification of the technique or rescheduling of the DEXA test may be required in the following situations: Recently administered gastrointestinal contrast or radionuclides Pregnancy Scoliosis, which may affect spine measurements Severe degenerative changes or fracture deformity in the measurement area Implants, hardware, devices, or other foreign material in the measurement area The patient's inability to attain the correct position or remain motionless for the measurement Extremes of high or low BMI may adversely affect the ability to obtain accurate and precise measurements. Quantitative computed tomography (QCT) may be a desirable alternative for these individuals. Any condition that precludes proper positioning of the patient to be able to obtain accurate BMD values

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Pediatric Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Nuclear medicine scans are very safe as the radiopharmaceutical contains a trace amount of the biological compound and therefore has no pharmacological effect. Contraindications would relate to the appropriateness of the test ordered according to the latest guidelines and appropriateness use criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Angulation
retrieved_docs.content: Angulation can generally be thought of as the inclination at which the patient is positioned during a chest radiograph. An example of when a patient may have altered angulation is during a bedside chest radiograph when the bed's head is raised. This type of radiograph is called an apical lordotic view or a lordotic projection. These images can be especially useful in evaluating suspected lung pathology at the apices of the lungs. However, the distortion of several other structures in this view can lead to diagnostic errors. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging of the body is often complicated by the fact that anatomic structures overlap each other. Diagnostic accuracy of radiographs generally refers to how well an exam can predict a disease or condition's presence (or absence). The technologist is pivotal in improving diagnostic accuracy by providing diagnostic images. [1] This requires a technologist to know the various positions and techniques required to isolate and provide a clearer view of a body part being imaged. In addition to better viewing an anatomic part, different projections help anatomize an abnormality or localize a foreign body. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference that can be expanded into more detailed computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a CT of the chest and abdomen and a CT of the chest are directly mapped, indicating a direct and principal relationship between the plain radiograph and these focused CT studies. Additionally, the routine chest x-ray is linked as an alternative or supplementary reference for broader CT examinations, including a computerized tomography of the brain and chest as well as a computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen. These mappings illustrate how a basic chest x-ray can inform and guide the selection of more comprehensive CT imaging of multiple thoracic and cranial regions, supporting nuanced diagnostic decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging study that can be directly linked to several more advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is primarily mapped to both “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” and “Computerized tomography of chest,” indicating that these comprehensive and focused chest CT scans are the main follow‑up modalities when further anatomic detail is required beyond the plain radiograph. Additionally, routine chest x-ray is associated with other, less primary CT options, including “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium.” These alternative mappings reflect situations where clinicians may extend evaluation to include intracranial structures or require contrast enhancement for enhanced tissue characterization. Together, these relationships illustrate how a routine chest x-ray can guide the selection of targeted or extensive CT imaging, supporting diagnostic decision‑making and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: is a common extension of the basic abdominal protocol. Additional sub‑types—“Computerized tomography of chest,” “Computerized tomography of chest, abdomen and pelvis,” and “Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen”—are defined as children of the generic CT concept, reinforcing their status as specialized branches of the overall modality. Routine chest x‑ray serves as a foundational reference for many of these advanced studies, being the primary mapping source for chest‑focused and multi‑region CT examinations, including those that incorporate the brain. This structured network clarifies how basic radiographic exams (routine chest x‑ray and pelvic/abdominal CT) act as reference points for a wide array of specialized, often contrast‑enhanced, CT protocols across the chest, abdomen, pelvis and brain, facilitating clinicians’ understanding of protocol relationships, diagnostic pathways, and staging frameworks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2375
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is performed using plain X‑ray techniques. Computerized tomography of chest is considered the primary imaging modality that is mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating that chest CT often follows or expands upon findings from a standard chest radiograph. In addition, computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen is another imaging study that is also mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, representing an alternative or supplementary advanced imaging approach that can be pursued when a broader anatomic assessment is required. These relationships illustrate how plain X‑ray techniques underlie routine chest x‑ray, which in turn serves as a reference point for both focused chest CT and more extensive CT examinations of the brain, chest, and abdomen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a reference point for several more advanced computed tomography examinations. Both Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative mappings derived from a routine chest x-ray, indicating that these comprehensive CT studies may be ordered when a standard chest x-ray is insufficient. Additionally, Computerized tomography of chest is identified as the primary mapping from a routine chest x-ray, reflecting its role as the most direct CT extension of the basic chest radiograph. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate imaging modalities, progressing from a routine chest x-ray to targeted CT imaging of the chest alone or combined brain‑chest assessment, with or without contrast, based on diagnostic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1060
retrieved_docs.content: “rentgenografie” holds a broader relationship to the “routine chest x‑ray,” further integrating conventional radiography into the imaging hierarchy. Clinically, “ct scan abdomen” is associated with “تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية” (abdominal ultrasound) through a “clinically_associated_with” relationship, highlighting the complementary use of ultrasound alongside CT for abdominal assessment. Overall, the network portrays a hierarchical and mapping structure in diagnostic imaging: routine chest X‑ray serves as the foundational modality; plain X‑ray techniques and radiography (“rentgenografie”) expand from this base; tomographic methods (“tomografie”) and specific CT protocols (including contrast‑enhanced studies of chest, abdomen, pelvis, and brain) are derived or mapped from the routine chest X‑ray; and abdominal CT occupies a central position, linked both to broader imaging families and to complementary ultrasound, reflecting an integrated approach to patient imaging and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: The routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for more extensive imaging examinations, with both computerized tomography of the brain and chest and computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen being considered alternative or mapped extensions of the routine chest x-ray. This relationship highlights how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a basis for selecting broader CT studies that include additional anatomical regions such as the brain, chest, and abdomen, informing clinical decisions about when more detailed cross‑sectional imaging may be warranted.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2907
retrieved_docs.content: The routine chest x‑ray serves as an initial imaging reference that can lead to several more detailed computed tomography (CT) examinations. A CT of the chest, abdomen and pelvis is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating that findings on the plain radiograph often prompt a comprehensive CT evaluation of these three regions. In addition, other CT protocols that may be considered based on a routine chest x‑ray include a computerized tomography of the brain and chest, as well as a computerized tomography of the brain, chest and abdomen performed with intravenous contrast medium. These relationships illustrate how a simple chest radiograph can guide the selection of extensive cross‑sectional imaging to assess thoracic, abdominal, pelvic, and even cerebral structures when clinically indicated, highlighting the chest x‑ray’s role as a gateway to targeted, multi‑region CT studies for thorough diagnostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 111

This integrated hierarchy enables clinicians to select appropriate modalities, understand methodological bases, and navigate relationships among bone density testing, plain radiography, and CT across chest, abdomen, pelvis, retroperitoneum, genitourinary, and central nervous system for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2280
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference that is directly linked to several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating that the routine chest x-ray is the main baseline study informing the chest CT. In addition, the same routine chest x-ray is used as an alternative mapping source for more extensive CT studies, including Computerized tomography of brain and chest as well as Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen. These connections illustrate how a simple chest radiograph can guide the selection and interpretation of advanced cross‑sectional imaging of the chest alone or in combination with the brain and abdomen, supporting comprehensive diagnostic assessment and patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 464
retrieved_docs.content: Computer assisted surgery (CAS) is categorized as a subfield of other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, and it is also placed under the domains of magnetic resonance imaging and other CT scan modalities, reflecting that CAS relies on and integrates imaging technologies from radiology, MRI, and CT to guide and enhance surgical procedures.
